questions.prompt: A user reports a call connected but they couldn't hear the other side. CDR shows ANSWERED with a real Call Duration. You pull a SIP trace and the 200 OK's SDP body has c=IN IP4 192.168.1.50. The other party is on a public-Internet softphone. What's the likely root cause?
choices: Codec mismatch between the two endpoints
The trunk's DTMF mode is set to Inband
The SDP advertises an RFC 1918 (private) address that the public-Internet endpoint can't route to
The trunk's Maximum Concurrent Calls cap is being hit
questions.explanation: 192.168.1.50 is private. The other endpoint on the public Internet has no route to that address, so RTP packets head to the void. Fix: set the trunk's 'Select which IP address to use in Contact and Connection fields' to custom_ip with the public IP, or enable NAT on the extension.

questions.prompt: Put the diagnostic ladder in the correct order for a failed call.
items: Find the CDR row, read Status and Last Participant
Open Timeline View on that row, follow the chronological events
Pull a SIP trace from the troubleshooting menu, reproduce the call
Pull a PCAP with the Ethernet Capture Tool and open in Wireshark
questions.explanation: Climb the ladder only as far as you need to. Most calls are diagnosed at step 1 or 2 (CDR + Timeline). SIP trace handles cases where Timeline doesn't show why. PCAP is the wire-level deep dive, used only when SIP trace doesn't answer it.

questions.prompt: A trunk that shows 'Registered' in the trunk list status is guaranteed to be passing calls correctly.
questions.explanation: Registered means SIP signalling and authentication succeeded. It says nothing about codec compatibility, media-path NAT, or carrier-side blocks. A registered trunk can still produce one-way audio or rejected calls. Always test inbound and outbound end-to-end after a trunk change.
